chunks.text: An embedding is a numerical representation of text, images, audio, video, or other data in the form of a high-dimensional vector. Embeddings capture the semantic meaning of data so that similar items are represented by nearby vectors.
Embedding models transform unstructured data into dense numerical vectors. These vectors enable machines to compare the meaning of documents instead of relying solely on exact keyword matches.
Embeddings are widely used in semantic search, recommendation systems, Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG), document clustering, duplicate detection, image search, and question-answering systems.
Traditional keyword search relies on exact word matching, whereas semantic search compares embeddings to identify documents with similar meaning even when different words are used.
High-dimensional vectors may contain hundreds or thousands of numerical values. For example, embedding models commonly generate vectors with dimensions such as 384, 768, 1024, or 1536.
Popular embedding models include BGE-M3, BGE Large, Sentence Transformers, E5, OpenAI text-embedding models, Nomic Embed, Jina Embeddings, Cohere Embed, and multilingual embedding models.
The same embedding model should generally be used for both document embeddings and query embeddings. Using different embedding models may produce vectors that are not directly comparable.
A vector database is a specialized database designed to store embeddings and perform efficient similarity searches over millions or even billions of vectors.
Unlike relational databases that search using SQL conditions, vector databases retrieve information by measuring the similarity between vectors representing the user's query and stored documents.
Popular vector databases include FAISS, ChromaDB, Pinecone, Qdrant, Milvus, Weaviate, LanceDB, Elasticsearch Vector Search, Redis Vector Search, and Vespa.
FAISS is an open-source vector search library developed by Meta. It is commonly used for local vector search and research projects where a lightweight solution is sufficient.
ChromaDB is an open-source vector database designed specifically for AI applications. It provides an easy-to-use interface for storing embeddings, metadata, and documents.
Pinecone is a fully managed cloud vector database that automatically handles indexing, scaling, replication, and high-performance similarity search.
Qdrant is an open-source vector database that supports metadata filtering, distributed deployment, payload storage, and efficient Approximate Nearest Neighbor (ANN) search.
Milvus is a scalable open-source vector database optimized for handling billions of vectors and supporting enterprise-scale AI applications.
Each vector stored in a vector database is typically associated with metadata such as document ID, title, author, page number, category, source file, language, and creation date.
Metadata filtering allows searches to be restricted to a subset of documents. For example, searches can be limited to a specific department, product, language, publication year, or document type.
Similarity search identifies vectors that are closest to the query vector. Instead of returning exact matches, it retrieves documents that are semantically similar to the user's question.

Cosine similarity measures the angle between two vectors. A higher cosine similarity score indicates that the vectors point in similar directions and therefore represent semantically similar information.
Euclidean distance measures the straight-line distance between vectors. Smaller distances indicate greater similarity.
Dot product similarity measures the mathematical product of two vectors. It is frequently used when embeddings are normalized or when embedding models are trained using dot-product objectives.
Most modern vector databases use Approximate Nearest Neighbor (ANN) search rather than Exact Nearest Neighbor search because ANN significantly improves search speed while maintaining high accuracy.
Popular ANN indexing algorithms include HNSW (Hierarchical Navigable Small World), IVF (Inverted File Index), Product Quantization (PQ), ScaNN, and DiskANN.
HNSW builds a graph-based index that enables extremely fast similarity searches while maintaining excellent recall. It is one of the most commonly used ANN algorithms.
IVF partitions vectors into clusters before searching. During retrieval, only the most relevant clusters are searched, reducing query latency.
Product Quantization compresses vectors to reduce memory usage while still allowing efficient approximate similarity searches over very large datasets.
Before embeddings are generated, documents are typically preprocessed by removing unnecessary formatting, cleaning text, splitting into chunks, and attaching useful metadata.
Chunking divides large documents into smaller pieces before embedding generation. Proper chunk size improves retrieval quality because each embedding represents a focused topic.
Chunk overlap helps preserve context between neighboring chunks. Many RAG systems use overlapping chunks to avoid losing important information near chunk boundaries.
When a user submits a query, the same embedding model converts the question into a vector. The vector database then retrieves the top-k most similar document embeddings.
The retrieved documents are often reranked using cross-encoder models before being passed to a Large Language Model. Reranking improves the relevance of the final context.

Hybrid search combines semantic vector search with keyword-based search algorithms such as BM25. This approach improves retrieval when users search for exact product names, error codes, or technical terminology.

Vector databases are commonly used in enterprise search, recommendation engines, chatbots, customer support, legal research, medical knowledge systems, fraud detection, e-commerce search, and Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG).
Performance in vector databases depends on embedding quality, chunk size, indexing algorithm, metadata filtering, hardware resources, and the number of stored vectors.

Common challenges include duplicate embeddings, poor chunking strategies, inconsistent metadata, outdated knowledge bases, embedding drift after model upgrades, and balancing retrieval accuracy with search speed.

Best practices for vector search include using high-quality embedding models, maintaining clean metadata, selecting an appropriate chunk size, using overlap when necessary, evaluating retrieval quality regularly, and rebuilding indexes after significant data updates.
